results.title: Global Investment Trends Monitor, No. 50 - UNCTAD
results.snippet:
Investment patterns point to widening divides between developed and developing economies, growing concentration in a handful of strategic sectors and persistent weakness in projects most critical for sustainable development.

#### Growth driven by financial centres

More than $140 billion of the increase in global FDI came from higher flows through global financial centres. Without these “conduit flows”, global FDI rose by only about 5%, underlining the limited recovery in underlying investment activity.

Indicators of investor sentiment remained weak.

Global Investment trends, 2025 vs 2024
Global Investment trends, 2025 vs 2024

The message is clear: headline growth overstates the recovery. Policymakers should focus on reviving real investment, not just financial flows. [...] #### Developed economies pull ahead as developing countries fall behind

FDI flows to developed economies jumped 43% to $728 billion in 2025, driven by Europe and financial hubs. The European Union recorded a 56% increase, supported by large cross-border acquisitions and a rebound in major economies including Germany, France and Italy.

By contrast, flows to developing economies declined by 2% to $877 billion, accounting for 55% of global FDI. Lower-income countries were hit hardest. Three quarters of least developed countries saw stagnant or declining flows.

Foreign direct investment  trends, by reional groups, 2025 vs 2024
Foreign direct investment  trends, by reional groups, 2025 vs 2024 [...] Fran

results.title: [PDF] Changing patterns of capital flows - Bank for International Settlements
results.snippet: GFC, followed by a higher-frequency review of developments during the Covid-19 crisis of 2020. Throughout this chapter and the rest of the report, the focus is mainly on inflows to EMEs because fluctuations in gross flows have generally had larger economic and financial effects, and thus posed greater challenges, for these economies than for AEs. 1.1 Trends in aggregate flows Net versus gross flows Since the GFC, analysis of capital flows and their economic consequences has changed focus from net flows to gross flows.1 Emphasis used to be placed on net flows because they make up the financial counterpart to the current account balance. As such, they provide a convenient gauge of the impact of capital flows on a country’s real economy, asset prices and exchange rates. Net flows are also a [...] (2017)). In their replies to the survey conducted for the purpose of this report (Box 1A), central banks singled out the declining role of banks as the most important structural change in global patterns of capital flows. The growing role of asset managers, investment funds and other non-bank financial intermediaries was the second structural shift most frequently cited by survey respondents. results.title: [PDF] International Capital Flow Pressures and Global Factors
results.snippet: of risk sentiment (Bekaert, Engstrom and Xu 2021), the euro V STOXX index, and the RORO (Risk-On Risk-Off) series (Chari, Stedman and Lundblad 2020). [...] 4 The EMP, Risk Sentiment, and the Global Factor International capital flow pressures are driven by global factors or advanced economy push factors and by local pull factors. A long history of studies of capital flow drivers, and the influential work of Miranda-Agrippino and Rey (2015), point to a large and important global factor particularly associated with US monetary policy and risk sentiment. Some studies point to a close relationship between US monetary policy and risk sentiment (Kalemli-¨ Ozcan 2019). Other studies keep these drivers separate and find for a reduced role of risk sentiment via the V IX as reflecting the 19 (a) Colombia (b) China (c) Thailand (d) Switzerland Figure 3: Individual Components of the EMP (2005-2020) Presented are the EMP series with colors indicating the [...] 18 3.4 Monthly EMP Series with Components, Using Reference Currencies The resulting EMP values vary across countries and time. Four specific country examples il-lustrate these points: Colombia using a variety of tools in response to international capital flow pressures; China, heavily utilizing FXI and later allowing greater contributions of exchange rate movements; Thailand as an emerging market applying FXI to avoid either appreciation or de-preciation; and Switzerland as an advanced country that has actively used all three respect

results.snippet: suggests nearshoring interest remains high. We expect strong incentives for deep economic integration with the United States to prevail, boosting inflows in the long term. In Chile, robust FDI in the mining sector amid still-elevated copper prices should support inflows. Outflows (particularly from pension funds) will remain steady as investors continue diversifying abroad. In Colombia, capital flows are expected to remain stable given improving growth and prospects of pro-market policies in 2026. FDI should moderate due to volatile commodity prices and sluggish mining and energy investment. We expect portfolio inflows to persist, driven by sizable public financing needs, although high borrowing costs underscore elevated sovereign risk. Overall, LatAm is well-positioned to face global [...] to resilience, the underlying message is one of growing differentiation across asset types, regional blocks, and policy regimes. Portfolio flows are expected to reach $242 bn in 2025 and $247 bn in 2026 (Exhibit 5), reflecting a steady but selective recovery from $220 bn in 2024 and $158 bn in 2023. These flows are being driven almost entirely by EMs outside China, where local currency debt remains the dominant asset class. Countries like Brazil, Mexico, India, and South Africa are benefiting from real yield premia and improving inflation dynamics. Hard currency flows remain subdued, a function of higher global interest rates and increased external funding costs. On the equity side, invest

results.title: From Transition to Transformation: Global Capital Trends Shaping 2026 - Delphos
results.snippet:
Taken together, these transactions show how capital is shifting in Emerging Markets in 2026 toward assets that offer resilience, productivity, and long-term economic relevance. Africa’s growth in renewable power and data infrastructure, Asia’s investment in cross-border grids and logistics, and Latin America’s move to clean energy and private credit highlight a landscape defined by structural change. As global investors seek reliable returns in a low-growth environment, Delphos demonstrates how targeted financing and technology-enabled infrastructure can support sustainable growth and shape global capital flows for years to come. [...] Custom Featured Image

As we look toward 2026, the global financial landscape is not merely in transition; it is undergoing a fundamental transformation. The investment landscape is evolving rapidly, presenting new challenges and opportunities for investors. For investors, business owners, and financial institutions focused on Emerging Markets, understanding the currents driving this change is critical. The convergence of multipolar economic forces and a shifting geopolitical environment, along with artificial intelligence and a growing imperative for climate adaptation, is reshaping capital flows and creating new frontiers for investment. The coming year will test which investors can combine resilience with creativity, as that is where opportunity will emerge. [...] Finally, adaptation finance is moving from the periphery of policy discussion

results.title: Capital Flows Tracker | IIF
results.snippet:
## Capital Flows Tracker

The IIF Capital Flows Tracker includes all of our current portfolio flows data (previously included in our Portfolio Flows Tracker) as well as the broader net capital flow estimates. It is released near the end of each month, and both data sets (portfolio flows and net capital flows) are available for download below. Estimates in the tracker will also feed into our quarterly EM Capital Flows Report.

## Recent Trackers

results.snippet: 20, 2026. We believe these changes could ultimately help build a more durable foundation for global growth and support capital flows, yet the adjustment process is likely to be uneven and marked by shocks. We offer these five pieces of advice to long-term investors to manage through the transition: • Prepare for a potential continuing rally in emerging markets Emerging markets outperformed their DM peers by 11 percentage points (ppt) in 2025 but still lagged by 500 basis points (bps) annually over the last five years. The slog for EM may be over. Structural foundations are translating into earnings delivery. In calendar year 2026, EM corporate earnings are expected to grow 46%, while EM P/E multiples have corrected to 11.8x, still below longer-term historical averages. EM sovereign bond [...] cycle can continue through the rest of 2026. After the fourth-quarter earnings reporting season (January and February 2026), the five major hyperscalers (Microsoft, Meta, Oracle, Google and Amazon) lifted their capex expectations for 2026 by $130 billion. Wall Street analysts now expect they will spend more than $650 billion together through the end of 2026, the majority of that to expand their cloud-based AI capacity. Even Oracle – which has been scrutinized by market analysts for pursuing a debt- and equity-financed capital investment strategy – recently reported $30 billion of growth in its order book, entirely from customers that are paying for their own GPUs.87 Oracle may not have t

results.title: Instagram
results.snippet:
The global investment market continued to show resilience in Q1 2026, with investment activity moving in a positive direction despite ongoing macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty.  
  
As investors navigate a more complex environment, larger and more liquid markets continue to attract capital, while sector and regional strategies become increasingly focused and selective. Explore our latest Global Capital Flows report by clicking the link in our bio.  
  
Key takeaways from the report:  
➡️ EMEA remains the leading destination for cross-border capital, with the UK ranked first globally.   
➡️ Office and living gained market share in EMEA, while senior living jumped from 4% to 11% of Q1 activity. [...] ➡️ Although interest rates are expected to expand over the second half of 2026, current yield spreads provide a strong income buffer, supporting investment activity.   
➡️ Spain, Sweden and Poland saw positive GDP forecast revisions, while larger European economies were cut back.  
  
#ColliersIndonesia #ColliersGlobalCapitalMarkets #GlobalCapitalFlows #MarketInsights #CRE

results.title: Emerging Markets Capital Flows 2026: Three Structural Shifts
results.snippet:
H1 2026 produced three structural reconfigurations in Emerging Markets capital flows 2026 that every EM sponsor preparing a capital raise in H2 needs to understand before approaching investors. This blog is written for CFOs and heads of corporate finance at established businesses in built assets, logistics, energy, and critical minerals across Sub-Saharan Africa, Latin America, and Southeast Asia. It names the three reconfigurations, identifies the geographies, sectors, and economic growth drivers each favors, and outlines the capital-raising implications for sponsors operating in each context. The framework applies to debt and equity raises from US$50MM upward. [...] ### EM Selectivity Is Rising: What Sponsors in Frontier Markets Must Prove

The IMF revised its Emerging Markets growth forecast to 3.9% for 2026, down from 4.2% in January, with EM sovereign spreads excluding China projected to increase by 100 basis points under geopolitical stress scenarios, a macro backdrop that is now the baseline for every Emerging Markets capital flows conversation in H2 2026. The IMF Geopolitical Risk Index reflects the same dynamic shaping Emerging Markets capital flows: elevated and rising geopolitical stress is no longer an exception to the operating conditions; it is the permanent condition. [...] Real assets, and power capacity in particular, have become the dominant allocation theme among capital providers in 2026. Three forces are behind this: AI infrastructure demand is creating p

sections.title: Regulatory Shifts
sections.answer:
**Conclusion:** 2026 is seeing a coordinated global push that tightens AI governance, expands digital‑asset oversight, and broadens the regulatory perimeter to include fintech and non‑bank service providers.

- “Regulatory change in 2026 is heavily focused on AI governance, digital assets and payments, operational resilience, and recalibrating the prudential burden on banks and other financial institutions” [Web: citrincooperman.com]  
- “Supervisors are widening the regulatory perimeter to capture fintechs, critical third parties, and new crypto activities” [Web: citrincooperman.com]  
- “SEC and FINRA moving toward greater accommodation of cryptocurrencies within mainstream compliance frameworks” [Web: weaver.com]  
- “Continued pro‑digital‑assets policy … tokenization of real‑world assets … artificial intelligence usage” highlighted in the “2026 Regulatory Outlook” [Web: bbh.com]  
- “CFTC will continue allowing futures exchanges to list new kinds of contracts, including digital‑asset derivatives, event contracts and spot purchases and sales of digital assets” [Web: clearymawatch.com]  

These developments collectively reshape the operating landscape for international financial centers, asset managers, fintech firms, and digital‑asset businesses.

results.title: 2026 Market, Regulatory and Technology Pressures: Asset Management Risk Insights | Weaver
results.snippet:
### 2026 Strategic Outlook

As asset managers move further into 2026, proactive governance and technology oversight will be essential to meeting evolving regulatory expectations and managing emerging risks. Developments over the past year signal that private fund and digital asset considerations are no longer viewed as niche areas of oversight but have become mainstream components of regulatory examinations and risk management frameworks. [...] In parallel, blockchain-based issuance-to-servicing platforms for private funds were launched in 2025, facilitating early end-to-end transactions spanning issuance, settlement and servicing. Regulatory acceptance also continued to broaden globally, with both the SEC and FINRA moving toward greater accommodation of cryptocurrencies within mainstream compliance frameworks and Luxembourg’s regulator approving a fully tokenized fund on a public blockchain.

While near-term volatility remains a defining feature of the digital asset market, these infrastructure and regulatory developments suggest that digital assets and tokenization are becoming increasingly embedded within traditional asset management operations rather than remaining on the periphery. [...] In addition, advisers should continue to focus on allocation conflict management, valuation and liquidity practices for private and illiquid assets, and compliance with Regulation S-P’s enhanced privacy and safeguarding requirements. Firms offering private strategies to a broader investo

results.title: Regulatory Changes Affecting Financial Services in 2026 - Read More
results.snippet:
Image 2

Image 3

Image 4

Image 5Image 6

Image 7: Practical ERP Solutions Background

Regulatory change in 2026 is heavily focused on AI governance, digital assets and payments, operational resilience, and recalibrating the prudential burden on banks and other financial institutions. Supervisors are widening the regulatory perimeter to capture fintechs, critical third parties, and new crypto activities, even as some jurisdictions pursue targeted deregulation to support competitiveness and innovation.

## Cross‑Cutting Regulatory Changes in 2026 [...] ## Cross‑Cutting Regulatory Changes in 2026

Regulators are concentrating on four global shifts: AI governance, digital assets and payments, climate/ESG, and systemic interconnectedness between banks and non‑banks. While high‑level standards remain global, implementation is fragmenting, with the U.S., EU, UK, and Asia–Pacific taking distinct approaches to risk, growth, and innovation.

Authorities are also expanding the regulatory perimeter to cover crypto asset firms, buy‑now‑pay‑later providers, and critical third‑party tech vendors, bringing them under conduct, prudential, and resilience requirements. This shift means activities once considered “unregulated” are increasingly subject to licensing, reporting, and supervisory scrutiny.

## Banking and Prudential Regulation [...] At the same time, regulators are hardening requirements around information and communication technologies (ICT) and cyber risk, building on recent outa

results.title: 2026 Digital Assets Regulatory Update: A Landmark 2025 . . . But ...
results.snippet:
Fintechs and traditional financial institutions will likely continue to develop new products and services related to digital assets and distributed ledger technology, including new stablecoins; tokenized deposits, securities and other real world assets; new prime brokerage, cross-margining and other financing arrangements and complex derivatives and financial products tied to digital assets and tokenized instruments. [...] Notwithstanding the confirmation of the new CFTC Chairman and anticipated nominations of further Commissioners, we expect the CFTC to continue allowing futures exchanges to list new kinds of contracts, including digital asset derivatives, event contracts and spot purchases and sales of digital assets. The CFTC will also likely explore further ways for commodity brokers, swap dealers and derivatives clearing organizations to accept crypto and tokenized cash and securities as collateral for regulatory purposes, as well as for retail customers to access clearing organizations with no or more limited intermediation. [...] We expect there to be further proliferation of decentralized exchanges (DEXs) and decentralized finance (DeFi) protocols that may offer new venues to transact in both digital and traditional financial assets. In addition, there may be more venues and operators seeking to offer retail clients disintermediated access to financial markets. We may also see expanded roles for non-custodial wallet providers.
 Fintechs and traditional financial insti

## Insights

# Digital asset regulation accelerates in 2026

Digital Digest March 2026

Preface

In this issue

Digital assets through an institutional lens: A conversation with Angus Fletcher

SEC regulation of tokenized securities: Implications for asset managers and owners

Digital asset regulation accelerates in 2026

Image 4: Digital asset regulation march 2026

March 2026

Justin McCormackHead of Legal, Digital Assets Solutions [...] Justin McCormackHead of Legal, Digital Assets Solutions

For much of the past decade, the global regulatory posture toward digital assets has been defined by uneven progress: clear rules in some markets, uncertainty (and frequent enforcement-by-analogy) in others. In 2025, the tone shifted meaningfully. What changed was not that regulators “solved” digital assets. Rather, more jurisdictions moved from “what” to “how.”

Three key themes repeatedly surfaced: (i) clearer licensing and conduct expectations for intermediaries; (ii) more explicit approaches to digital money (stablecoins, tokenized deposits, and related settlement mechanisms); and (iii) continued efforts to align global standards on prudential treatment and finan

sections.title: Performance Alerts
sections.answer:
The most urgent issues are two initiatives lagging behind schedule and all five core KPIs falling short of their Q2 2026 targets.

- **CSO Intelligence Dashboard – “Behind”** (30 % progress, vendor change caused a 6‑week delay; new delivery date 15 Sep 2026) [Doc: initiative_status_report_q2_2026.pdf, p.1]  
- **Core Registry Upgrade – “Behind”** (15 % progress, procurement extended by security review; revised kick‑off 1 Aug 2026) [Doc: initiative_status_report_q2_2026.pdf, p.1]  
- **Digital Asset Licensing Fast‑Track – “At Risk”** (45 % progress, consultant delivery delayed 3 weeks; SLA still pending) [Doc: initiative_status_report_q2_2026.pdf, p.1]  
- **Talent Attraction Programme – “At Risk”** (40 % progress, visa processing delays averaging 47 days) [Doc: initiative_status_report_q2_2026.pdf, p.1]  
- **KPIs off‑track** – all five metrics below target: New Entity Registrations 356 vs 380; Assets Under Management USD 171 bn vs 175 bn; Licensed Fintech Firms 109 vs 120; Digital Asset Licenses 28 vs 35; Workforce 1,310 FTE vs 1,350 [Doc: board_strategy_memo_q2_2026.pdf, p.1]

sections.title: Risk Indicators
sections.answer:
**Conclusion:** Our internal risk registers highlight several at‑risk initiatives and infrastructure gaps, while external intelligence points to evolving regulatory mandates, heightened Middle‑East geopolitical tension, and broader market volatility that could affect the center’s stability.

- **Internal risk flags:** Regulatory arbitrage risk – “Singapore MAS proposed a 30‑day fast‑track license for crypto custodians — response required” [Doc: board_strategy_memo_q2_2026.pdf, p.2]; Talent pipeline – “Senior compliance roles remain unfilled for an average of 97 days” [Doc: board_strategy_memo_q2_2026.pdf, p.2]; Technology infrastructure – “Core registry system requires upgrade before Q4 2026 to support projected volume” [Doc: board_strategy_memo_q2_2026.pdf, p.2]; Digital Asset Licensing Fast‑Track – “Consultant delivery delayed by 3 weeks… Risk of missing the Q3 2026 go‑live target” [Doc: initiative_status_report_q2_2026.pdf, p.1]; Talent Attraction Programme – “Visa processing delays averaging 47 days are deterring senior international candidates” [Doc: initiative_status_report_q2_2026.pdf, p.1]; CSO Intelligence Dashboard – “Technology vendor change mid‑project caused a 6‑week delay” [Doc: initiative_status_report_q2_2026.pdf, p.1]; Core Registry Upgrade – “Procurement process extended due to additional security review requirements” [Doc: initiative_status_report_q2_2026.pdf, p.1].

- **External regulatory developments to monitor:** ISSB S1/S2 Sustainability Disclosures – “Deadline 30 Sep 2026 — High — Draft guidance published” [Doc: regulatory_compliance_summary_2026.pdf, p.1]; FATF AML/CFT Framework Update – “Deadline 15 Aug 2026 — High — Training underway” [Doc: regulatory_compliance_summary_2026.pdf, p.1]; Digital Asset Custody Rules v2.0 – “Deadline 1 Oct 2026 — Medium — Consultation open” [Doc: regulatory_compliance_summary_2026.pdf, p.1]; Cross‑Border Payment Reporting – “Deadline 31 Jul 2026 — Medium — Mapping in progress” [Doc: regulatory_compliance_summary_2026.pdf, p.1]; Data Residency Requirements – “Deadline 1 Jan 2027 — High — IT scoping started” [Doc: regulatory_compliance_summary_2026.pdf, p.1].

- **Geopolitical risks:** “Geopolitical tensions remain a key variable as any escalation could introduce periods of short‑term volatility and disrupt market sentiment” [Web: ssga.com]; “Heightened geopolitical tensions are an increasing focus for financial regulators and central banks globally” [Web: rba.gov.au]; “The escalation of geopolitical tensions in the Middle East has triggered a sharp sell‑off in emerging market currencies” [Web: imf.org].

- **Market‑related risks:** “GCC equity markets remain sensitive to headline risk—particularly around energy prices and regional logistics” [Web: ssga.com]; “Increased uncertainty in financial markets… could undermine investor confidence and lead to heightened risk aversion” [Web: rba.gov.au]; “Sharp increase in risk premiums… triggered by dollar appreciation, rising global interest rates, or equity downturns” [Web: imf.org].

args.query: regulatory risks financial centers 2026 geopolitical tensions market volatility GCC financial center

results.title: The path ahead for GCC equities | State Street
results.snippet:
The region continues to demonstrate economic resilience: the GCC is expected to be relatively less impacted by global trade frictions and enjoy relatively robust growth, with governments in the region working on broadening trade and investment relationships.  
  
However, several factors could challenge GCC equities in 2026. Geopolitical tensions remain a key variable as any escalation could introduce periods of short term volatility and disrupt market sentiment. [...] Geopolitical conflicts typically lead to heightened volatility and short term drawdowns in equity markets, while history suggests they have limited impact on long term returns unless they evolve into sustained macroeconomic shocks. In the current environment, GCC equity markets remain sensitive to headline risk—particularly around energy prices and regional logistics—but have so far demonstrated relative resilience supported by strong fiscal positions and high oil revenues. [...] Historically, GCC equity performance has been closely linked to energy prices, reflecting the region’s role as a major oil and gas producer. Over the past decade, however, this relationship has moderated. The MSCI GCC Countries Combined Index—which captures approximately 85% of free-float-adjusted market capitalization across the six member states—illustrates a pattern of resilience and steadier returns, even amid volatile energy markets. This decoupling has become more evident in early 2026.

results.title: Geopolitical Risk and Financial Stability | Bulletin – June 2026 | RBA
results.snippet:
## Introduction

Heightened geopolitical tensions are an increasing focus for financial regulators and central banks globally.1 Recent conflicts in the Middle East and Ukraine, and intensifying cyber-attacks by state-sponsored actors, have also prompted financial institutions to increase their focus on geopolitical risks. In Australia, the Council of Financial Regulators (CFR) is working with industry to ensure the financial system is prepared for a range of geopolitical scenarios. [...] Increased uncertainty in financial markets, which could undermine investor confidence and lead to heightened risk aversion. This may result in increased market volatility, disorderly price declines and a surge in trading volumes, potentially testing the capacity of financial market infrastructure.
   Disruptions in access to funding, impacting financial institutions that rely on international markets. Sovereign bond spreads may also widen in response to expectations of higher fiscal spending and increased risk premia, making it more difficult for governments to access capital in a time of need. [...] ### Political risks

Political risks relate to the potential impact of international policy actions, including financial sanctions, capital controls and offshore asset seizures. Heightened geopolitical tension could result in broad-based or targeted sanctions being imposed rapidly by multiple jurisdictions. Differences in the scope, timing and design of sanctions regimes can create operational co

results.title: [PDF] Geopolitical Conflict and Financial Market Reactions
results.snippet: in capital movement and fluctuations in government bond markets (Curatola-Melo & Guimaraes, 2026). The study adopts a forward-looking geopolitical conflict framework involving the United States, Israel, and Iran during 2026 to examine potential financial market reactions under heightened geopolitical uncertainty. The Middle East holds vital importance because it controls fundamental routes for energy distribution, which powers the entire planet. The Strait of Hormuz serves as a critical chokepoint since it controls access to maritime routes through which a major portion of global oil shipments must move. The global oil markets react immediately to any disturbance or danger that threatens this transportation route. Regional tensions disrupt energy supply chains, which create energy price [...] disrupt energy supply chains, which create energy price fluctuations and lead to economic uncertainty throughout the world (Jazeera, 2026). The financial markets show their initial response to geopolitical tensions through oil price movements, which serve as primary financial indicators. The markets begin to monitor two key elements when conflicts arise in important oil-producing nations: the potential production disruptions and the routes that carry energy supplies. The expectation itself leads to a major increase in oil prices (The Economist, 2026). The global economy experiences effects from rising oil prices, which impact inflation rates, transportation expenses, and economic develop

results.title: The Institute of International Finance > Events > Meeting
results.snippet:
The Institute of International Finance

# 2026 GCC Investor Trip

The IIF GCC Investor Trip will bring together private-sector leaders and officials from across the gulf region to assess the region's economic and political outlook amid the ongoing conflict with Iran. Discussions will focus on how the ongoing conflict with Iran is reshaping macroeconomic trajectories for 2026 and beyond, including growth, fiscal balances, and capital flows, as well as policy responses to rising geopolitical risks and oil market volatility.

results.title: [PDF] Global Financial Stability Report, April 2026, Chapter 1
results.snippet: rapid repricing. The escalation of geopolitical tensions in the Middle East has triggered a sharp sell-off in emerging market currencies, reversing part of the strong rally since early 2025. Relative foreign exchange perfor-mance since the start of the conflict has increasingly reflected differences in energy exposure. Currencies of more oil-import-dependent economies have gener-ally underperformed as markets price in terms of trade pressures and higher inflation risks (Figure 1.6, panel  3), while several earlier outperformers have experienced sharper reversals amid positioning [...] Emerging market assets retrace some recent strong gains amid escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East ... [...] quintiles of VIX (Figure 1.5, panel 4). This suggests investors’ use of derivatives to obtain leverage could leave emerging markets more vulnerable to global shocks than in the past. Ahead of the outbreak of the conflict in the Middle East, global investors’ desire for greater diversification and improvements in macroeconomic fundamentals supported appetite for emerging market assets, raising valuations and compressing risk premiums across interest rates, corporate credit, and equities (Figure 1.6, panel 1). A sharp increase in risk premiums is his-torically commonplace under the current conditions, triggered by dollar appreciation, rising global interest rates, or equity downturns (Figure 1.6, panel 2), rais-ing the scope for rapid repricing. The escalation of geopolitical
